title: Logigraph process node
description: One node = one business-process flow. Acts as an index: surfaces the code, UI, broadcasts, and side-effects a flow touches so an editor of any one piece can see the rest. NOT a tutorial — the dossier is optional and terse. Pointer semantics, not narrative.

summary.description: One-paragraph elevator pitch. What kicks it off, what it changes, what downstream consumers exist. Read at the top of the hook injection.

flow.description: How this flow gets kicked off. Every flow must carry an `action` describing the entry point in human terms — for user-initiated flows that means a user action ('User clicks X on Y'); for headless flows that means the system event ('Stripe webhook fires', 'Cron at 02:00 UTC'). UI + endpoint pointers are optional but recommended for user flows.

action.description: One-line description of what kicks off the flow. User-initiated flows MUST describe this as a user action (e.g. 'User clicks Accept on the invite landing page').

ui_surface.description: Optional depgraph node id of the UI element (component, page) the user interacts with to start the flow. Recommended for user-initiated flows; omit for headless triggers.

endpoint.description: Optional depgraph node id of the HTTP endpoint (or handler function) that receives the trigger. Typical for user actions: the API endpoint the UI calls.

steps.description: Ordered list of process steps. First step is implicitly the entry. Steps with no transitions are terminals.

ui_surfaces.description: Cross-repo UI dependents the hook should surface. Process-level (not per-step) by default; multi-page flows can override per-step in a future extension.

broadcasts.description: Websocket / event-bus event names this flow emits. Often invisible from depgraph edges; flagging them here lets the hook warn editors about silent downstream consumers.

state_modifications.description: Terse plain-English statements of intentional state changes the flow performs — DB writes, deletes, status flips, broadcasts the flow exists to emit. The flow's contract; editors should preserve these. Empty is fine if the flow is purely a lookup/query.

side_effects.description: Actual side effects — incidental or unintended consequences of running the flow that aren't the flow's purpose. Metrics counters, log lines, cache invalidations downstream, audit-trail writes. Usually empty; distinct from `state_modifications` which are the point. Flag here when something happens on the way that an editor might break or need to preserve unrelated to the flow's main contract.

enforces_rules.description: Rule ids this process is expected to enforce somewhere in its steps. Reverse-indexed by reconcile into rule.enforced_in_processes (derived, not authored).

dossier.description: Path to the optional dossier markdown (relative to LOGIGRAPH). Dossier is intentionally NOT required to carry structured sections — it's prose, used sparingly, often just "## Why this is a process and not N rules".

analyzed_by.description: Who/what performed the analysis behind this node (e.g. the LLM model id). Recorded distinctly from the human reviewer (flagged_by / dossier reviewed_by).

analyzed_at.description: ISO-8601 timestamp of when the analysis ran.

flagged.description: Set by `bin/logigraph flag`. true = this process documents a known deficiency or contested decision; injection surfaces a FLAGGED banner.

flagged_by.description: Who flagged. Written by `flag` from --actor or $USER.

id.description: Within-process step id. Stable; transitions reference these.

title.description: One-line what-this-step-does. The pointer label, not a paragraph.

claims_code.description: Depgraph nodes that implement this step. Mirrors rule.claims_code shape so reconcile's drift detection works the same way.

enforces_rules.description: Rule ids enforced AT this step. Per-step granularity so the hook can say "this step enforces rule X."

transitions.description: Outgoing edges. Empty / missing = terminal step. Multiple = branch (each carries a `when` predicate in prose). `to` is a step id within this process; reconcile validates the reference.

role.description: Why this code is claimed by this step. 'implements' = primary code that runs the step; 'enforces' = guard / invariant check; 'renders' = UI; 'triggers' = initiator; 'consumes' = downstream reaction.

How this UI piece participates in the flow. primary_entry = where user starts; service_invoker = the api-call wrapper; post_effect = UI that updates after success (WS handlers, refetch triggers); error_surface = handles failure modes.
